titleStmt.title: Letter from Wilfrid Spender to Lillian Spender, 28 November 1915
Letters 1916-1923
titleStmt.author: Wilfrid Spender

editionStmt.p: This work was originally published by Maynooth University in Ireland in 2017. In 2026 this data, stored in a relational database was extracted and converted into this TEI/XML document.

availability.p: This is an open access work licensed under Creative Commons Attribution NonCommercial 4.0 International license (CC BY-NC 4.0).

note.p: Letter from Sir Wilfrid Spender (1876 - 1960) to his wife, Lillian (1880-1968). Spender was a member of the British Army until 1913 when controversy surrounding his signature of the Ulster Solemn League and Covenant resulted in his resignation. Spender was called up for military service in 1914 and from 1915 served in the 36th (Ulster) Division. Throughout the war, Spender wrote a collection of letters to his wife Lady Lillian Spender. The letters date mainly from 1915 to 1918, when Sir Wilfrid was serving in France. The letters concern politics, the Irish question, personal life, and Wilfrid's experience of the Great War."

msIdentifier.repository: Public Record Office of Northern Ireland
msIdentifier.collection: Letters from Sir Wilfrid Spender to Lady Spender, D1633/1/1/80

projectDesc.p: The data in these XML files was generated based on a data dump from the Magellan database (https://github.com/Maynooth-Center-for-Digital-Humanities/Magellan). Each database record has been converted to a JSON file (https://github.com/letters1916static/letters-data/tree/main/json). The element section of the JSON file contains the TEI-encoded contents of the letters. The TEI XML has been cleaned and made well-formed using a Python script (https://github.com/letters1916static/letters-data/tree/main/src).

body.ab:
28. The PR. I think wolves turn grey & gaunt in the winter, but
p rs only turn more rosey, & gamble with squirrels, whilst the b w
cannot even find an ordinary r in this country. However perhaps
in the new place we may discover a delightful Contesse, & then. I am sorry you should write with a headache, do not
make a burden of it, but simply scamper a few paw
marks over the paper & shut up. I am not altogether satisfied with your Committee
work it seems to be getting rather in to an impasse, &
some of your co workers will not mind, I'm afraid. However
I've faith that you can square out something with Lady R
& Mrs Barr & smooth thing out. I'm rather glad you
should see a little of the relicts of the smart society way
but how absolutely nauseating all that dress flummery is.
I have written to Crichton that you are paying visits
but will hope to see him when you get settled down. There is a lot of news I want to hear from you
Our flat, your family ! migration, what Lady C thinks says
of my outburst to Sir E, your arrangements with Lady R
etc etc. I fancy K S must be like his wife. he was running (?)
an outpost scheme of the 14 RIR when the GOC went out
& watched. After 5 mins the battalion was ordered to march
home ! Gen C said he was not out to watch children's play. Our new "old Arty" is being detrained by Richardson
who will have rather a poor time of it I fear the next
48 hours. I wanted to relieve him, but had to be toed
off for other work. Gen' Brandon is not coming out. Too old I suppose!
We have rather a large division just now but I suppose our
Artywill leave shortly. Gen N is not at all keen about a separate Ulster
& would wilingly compromise with the Nationalist party
if it meant support by the Govt. There is no fervour about
his faith in this respect & he will want watching later
after the war. I think Sir E ought to know this. It
is not against him of course but simply that he has
quite different convictions to the stalwarts of the Covenant
& believes in the possibility of agreement. He showed his
true colours when L Leitrim Ricardo & I went for
him & tho he changed again at that time it was
not a conversion of mind. I'm sure old thing it was good for the Mater
to have you with her. Lately her tone has been very
much of the old age spirit & a desire for peace & rest.
It is so different from her old tone, but I do hope she
won't lose all her old fighting spirit, tho' there is something
fine in the change too. I really think you have
been a comfort to her, tho' I expect you have not quite
escaped all criticism. The Qnew Div taking over from us here ha s
been passing through & have some apositive bounders among
their COs. To think of England being flooded after the war
by all these Colonels ! My love to both families. You have hardly
told me a word of 2q's doings. Greedy little animal expecting to get all the
letters to yourself. As if Mater mightn't have 1
a week addressed to her. Oh p r ! All the same
I rather like you. Your b w WB Spender
